name: John Lee Hooker
abstract: John Lee Hooker (August 22, 1912 or 1917 – June 21, 2001) was an American blues singer, songwriter, and guitarist. The son of a sharecropper, he rose to prominence performing an electric guitar-style adaptation of Delta blues that he developed in Detroit.

concerts.label: John Lee Hooker and Canned Heat

works.t: It Serves You Right to Suffer

concerts.label: Boogie n' Blues at Carnegie Hall

works_created.t: It Serves You Right to Suffer

about_quotes.speaker: Red Holloway
about_quotes.evidence: John Lee Hooker’s one. I mean he may say “Boom Boom Boom” but he’s got that voice and he’s got his type of rhythm that he can do that and get away with it.
about_quotes.doc_title: Red Holloway interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, Scottsdale, Arizona, March 4, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Quinn Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: Yeah, John Lee Hooker. And we did a lot of recordings, back-up, with the Spanials,, Jerry Butler. This was all when we was with VeeJay.

about_quotes.speaker: Mose Allison
about_quotes.evidence: There were also the country blues people, like Muddy Waters, Lightnin’ Hopkins, and John Lee Hooker.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mose Allison — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Branford Marsalis
about_quotes.evidence: And then they had Howling Wolf singing his tunes. And John Lee Hooker singing his tunes.
about_quotes.doc_title: Branford Marsalis — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Albany Leon "Barney" Bigard
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Yeah, John Lee Hooker. [INTERVIEWEE] Yeah. Is he good?
